old_claim.note: Madrigals & Polyphonic Music provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Venetian public opera houses grew from earlier commercial theater infrastructure with purpose-built venues, boxes, seating, and civic safety oversight.

source_shape.source_locator: Our History: the San Cassiano site had a Tron family theater for comedies from 1581; the source describes theater boxes, purpose-built venues, structural-safety oversight, and the 1636 plan to open a theater for music before the 1637 public opera house.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source traces San Cassiano from prior public/commercial theater infrastructure into the first public opera house.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: That lineage fits the venue-infrastructure node better than madrigals, which are musical repertoire rather than public opera-house architecture or operations.

ontology_before: Opera House Infrastructure depended on Madrigals & Polyphonic Music, conflating musical repertoire lineage with venue infrastructure.
ontology_after: Opera House Infrastructure depends on Public Theater Infrastructure as the historical venue lineage for seating, boxes, safety oversight, and public operation.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: opera_house_infrastructure now depends on public_theater_infrastructure and no longer directly depends on madrigals_polyphonic_music.
would_reject_if: The node were rescoped from opera-house infrastructure to the musical origin of opera or madrigal style.
A stronger source showed madrigal/polyphonic repertoire was a direct infrastructure prerequisite for the first public opera house.
A dedicated Venetian public theater boxes node were introduced and should replace the broader public_theater_infrastructure edge.
short_reason: Opera-house infrastructure inherits public theater venue systems, not madrigal repertoire.
